title: Quasigeodesic pseudo-Anosov flows in hyperbolic 3-manifolds
speaker: Sergio Fenley
abstract: Workshop on Flows, Foliations and Contact Structures 2015-2016 Monday, December 7, 2015 - 08:00 to Friday, December 11, 2015 - 12:00 This workshop is part of the topical program "Geometric Structures on 3-Manifolds" which will take place during the 2015-2016 academic year at the Institute for Advanced Study. This workshop will focus on the relations between flows, 2-dimensional foliations, and contact structures on 3-manifolds.
tags: Workshop on Flows Foliations and Contact Structures

proof_stages.label: Pseudo-Anosov flow and quasigeodesic setup
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines pseudo-Anosov and Anosov flows in closed 3-manifolds via stable/unstable foliations with allowed p-prong singularities for p>=3, then formulates quasigeodesicity of lifted flow lines and the relevant uniformity property.

proof_stages.label: Free-homotopy obstruction via orbit-space intersections
proof_stages.summary: R-covered Anosov examples motivate the argument that if too many freely homotopic closed orbits were uniformly quasigeodesic, their lifts would be close enough to force stable/unstable intersections in the orbit space, producing two closed orbits in one stable leaf and a contradiction.

proof_stages.label: Auxiliary-foliation mechanisms for quasigeodesicity
proof_stages.summary: Known positive criteria use almost-transverse finite-depth foliations or regulating slithering foliations to control how flow lines cross leaves, showing quasigeodesicity by importing geometric information from a transverse foliation.

proof_stages.label: Bounded free-homotopy characterization
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is stated: a pseudo-Anosov flow in a closed hyperbolic 3-manifold is quasigeodesic iff each closed orbit is freely homotopic to at most a uniform number of other closed orbits, and the converse is recast under purely dynamical assumptions plus no nontrivial self-free-homotopy.

proof_stages.label: Orbit-space compactification and flow ideal boundary
proof_stages.summary: Using only the stable and unstable orbit-space foliations, the orbit space is compactified by an ideal circle and then quotiented to a flow ideal boundary, with freely homotopic orbits, lozenges, and perfect fits explaining why polygonal paths are needed.

proof_stages.label: Uniform convergence action and Bowditch identification
proof_stages.summary: The key technical step is to prove that the fundamental group acts as a uniform convergence group on the flow ideal boundary, after which Bowditch's theorem identifies the group as Gromov hyperbolic and the flow boundary as its sphere boundary.

proof_stages.label: Flow compactification endpoint criterion
proof_stages.summary: A compactification of the universal cover is shown to agree equivariantly with the Gromov compactification, and the well-behaved distinct continuous forward/backward endpoints of flow lines imply quasigeodesicity by a Fenley-Mosher criterion.

examples.name: Suspension of a pseudo-Anosov surface homeomorphism
examples.note: The suspension flow first gives a classical pseudo-Anosov example, then its cyclic-cover level surfaces explain quasigeodesicity and later model the ideal-circle compactification by escaping stable/unstable leaves.
examples.engagement_reason: he runs the suspension example through concrete arguments, suspending stable/unstable foliations and later proving quasigeodesicity via cyclic-cover levels and identifying its orbit space with a lifted fiber

passes.reason: A suspension mapping-torus flow is a standard infinite family and is used as a positive quasigeodesic example.

examples.name: Geodesic flow of a negatively curved surface
examples.note: The unit tangent bundle geodesic flow is recalled as the classical Anosov example and later illustrates why self-free-homotopies of closed orbits must be excluded in the generalized hypotheses.
examples.engagement_reason: he not only names the geodesic flow but uses a closed geodesic orbit in the unit tangent bundle to exhibit a self free-homotopy by rotating the tangent angle through 2π

passes.reason: A negatively curved surface geodesic flow is a standard Anosov family introduced as a primary example rather than a refutation.

examples.name: Goodman-Fried Dehn surgery constructions
examples.note: Surgeries on transverse annuli or closed orbits are used to illustrate that pseudo-Anosov and Anosov flows in hyperbolic 3-manifolds are abundant, with the prong count governed by the surgery intersection with the degeneracy locus.
examples.engagement_reason: he describes the Goodman-Fried surgery operation concretely, including blow-up/blow-down and how meridian intersections with stable prongs determine the resulting prong number

passes.reason: Goodman-Fried surgeries form a systematic Dehn-surgery family producing pseudo-Anosov or Anosov flows.
A systematic Dehn-surgery construction producing ordinary Anosov or pseudo-Anosov flows in hyperbolic manifolds.

examples.name: R-covered Anosov flows with infinite free-homotopy classes
examples.note: This counterexample class exposes the obstruction that infinitely many freely homotopic closed orbits contradict uniform quasigeodesicity, thereby motivating the bounded-free-homotopy condition in the theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: he analyzes R-covered Anosov flows to prove non-quasigeodesicity from infinite free-homotopy classes using lifted orbits, bounded distance, stable/unstable intersections, and a contradiction in the orbit space

passes.reason: These R-covered Anosov flows have infinite free-homotopy classes and are used to show quasigeodesicity can fail.

examples.name: Almost-transverse finite-depth foliation
examples.note: The finite-depth foliation case shows how quasi-Fuchsian compact leaves force lifted flow lines that repeatedly cross them to acquire unique ideal endpoints, giving a foliation-based mechanism for quasigeodesicity.
examples.engagement_reason: he sketches the finite-depth foliation argument, using quasifuchsian compact leaves, lifted quasidisks, repeated orbit intersections, and shrinking limit sets to get ideal-point control

passes.reason: Almost-transverse finite-depth foliations are a standard structured class used as a sufficient condition for quasigeodesicity.
A finite-depth foliation condition used to prove quasigeodesicity of the associated flow.

examples.name: Regulating flow transverse to a slithering foliation
examples.note: The slithering/regulating situation illustrates another positive criterion, where uniform Hausdorff control of leaves and mandatory crossings mimic the suspension argument.
examples.engagement_reason: he explains the regulating slithering case by defining the lifted intersection/product behavior and adapting the suspension distance argument to obtain quasigeodesicity

passes.reason: Regulating flows transverse to slithering foliations are a systematic product-like class used to prove quasigeodesicity.
